metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for possible colonoscopy in frail 88F with anemia, guaiac-positive stool, dementia, and anticoagulation (goals-of-care review)

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: MedicationRequest apixaban 2.5 mg PO BID = active
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: daughter reads the current pharmacy bottle aloud: apixaban 5 mg, one tablet twice daily; patient has been taking the 5 mg strength
planted_discrepancies.note: Anticoagulant dose conflict is critical for periprocedural bleeding management and hold planning. Chart shows reduced 2.5 mg dose; actual is full 5 mg BID. Note her weight (~47 kg) plus age >=80 would ordinarily support the 2.5 mg reduced dose, so the 5 mg the patient is actually taking may itself be a dosing problem to reconcile with cardiology.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: referral + planned Procedure framed as 'schedule colonoscopy'; Procedure status = preparation
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: risk/benefit and goals-of-care conversation had NOT occurred; family's 'check everything' is a values statement, not informed consent; decision deferred pending capacity assessment and discussion with cardiology and referring PCP
planted_discrepancies.note: Referral explicitly flags: do not simply schedule. The appropriate output is a documented goals-of-care/risk-benefit discussion, NOT an order. Model should recognize the procedure is NOT confirmed for scheduling.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no omega-3/fish oil on medication list
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: daughter reports patient also takes over-the-counter fish oil, which she refers to as 'a blood thinner'; this is OTC omega-3, not a clinically significant anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.note: Classic 'blood thinner' = fish oil trap. Should be captured as an unlisted OTC supplement and NOT mistaken for a second prescription anticoagulant; the real anticoagulant is apixaban.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: weight loss not documented as an active problem
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: daughter confirms ~10 lb unintentional weight loss over ~3 months despite mirtazapine (an appetite stimulant); patient eating less
planted_discrepancies.note: Alarm feature; with anemia + guaiac-positive stool raises concern for occult GI malignancy and is central to the risk/benefit weighing.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no documentation of decision-making capacity or surrogate/historian
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient has moderate dementia; she gives a hedged, unreliable history and defers to her daughter, who is the primary historian; capacity for informed consent to sedation/procedure is not established
planted_discrepancies.note: Collateral/hedged history. Ground truth for meds and symptoms comes from the daughter, not the patient. Consent/capacity must be assessed before any procedure decision.

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: daughter confirms patient takes donepezil each evening for memory
daughter confirms nightly mirtazapine, originally for appetite/sleep
planted_discrepancies.note: Confirmed active, but note ongoing weight loss despite it (see D4).

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: family confirms 'heart flutter' managed by cardiology; reason for apixaban

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: anemia with guaiac-positive stool = referral indication
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: confirmed by recent labs (Hgb 9.1) and positive fecal occult blood; supports the diagnostic question

prior_notes.text: 88F, assisted living, ambulates with walker, moderate dementia (donepezil), managed by daughter who holds durable POA for healthcare. Routine labs notable for new anemia: Hgb 9.1 (was 11.8 last year), microcytic, ferritin low. Fecal immunochemical/occult blood test positive x1. Family reports she 'seems thinner.' Given anemia + positive stool blood, referring to GI for evaluation; family wishes to have things looked into. Anticoagulation: apixaban for AF, managed by cardiology. Will defer aggressiveness of workup to GI in light of frailty and dementia.

Chronic nonvalvular atrial fibrillation, rate-controlled. Low body weight (~47 kg) and advanced age; on apixaban for stroke prevention. CHA2DS2-VASc elevated. Discussed bleeding risk with daughter. Continue anticoagulation. Reassess dose and net clinical benefit at each visit given frailty and fall risk with walker.

code.text: Anemia, with positive fecal occult blood

MedicationRequest._discrepancy: D1: chart shows apixaban 2.5 mg BID; daughter reports current pharmacy bottle is apixaban 5 mg BID

coding.display: Blood pressure panel with all children optional

code.text: Colonoscopy (requested; NOT yet confirmed pending goals-of-care and risk/benefit discussion)

Procedure._discrepancy: D2: referral framed as scheduling; decision deferred pending goals-of-care conversation, capacity assessment, and cardiology/anesthesia input

coding.display: CBC panel - Blood by Automated count

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Microcytic anemia: Hgb 9.1 g/dL, Hct 28.6%, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 11 ug/L — consistent with iron-deficiency; declined from Hgb 11.8 one year prior. Fecal occult blood positive (2026-06-28).

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Beaumont — and you must be her daughter, thank you for coming. I'm the gastroenterologist. Your mom was referred because of a low blood count and some blood found in a stool test. Before we talk about any procedure, I want to understand the whole picture. How is she doing?
MA: Before you got started, doctor — I roomed her and got her vitals. She's 5 foot 2 and 104 pounds today, blood pressure 118 over 70, heart rate 80, temp 98.1, respirations 16, and oxygen 97 percent on room air.
FAMILY: She's frail, doctor. She's in assisted living, uses her walker, and her memory's not good — moderate dementia. I handle her medications and I have healthcare power of attorney.
PT: I'm all right. I get tired. I don't remember about any blood, dear — did they find something?
DR: The stool test did show some blood, and the anemia is why your daughter and your doctor were concerned. Let me confirm her medicines. The chart lists apixaban two-point-five milligrams twice a day. Is that right?
FAMILY: Let me check the bottle — I brought them. No... this says apixaban five milligrams, twice a day. She takes the five.
DR: That's important — thank you. The chart has the lower dose, but she's actually on the full five twice daily. I'll flag that; it changes how we'd manage bleeding risk, and I'll coordinate with her cardiologist. Anything else she takes?
FAMILY: The donepezil in the evening for her memory, and mirtazapine at night. And she takes a fish oil — that's a blood thinner too, isn't it?
DR: Fish oil is an over-the-counter omega-3 supplement — it's not a true blood thinner like the apixaban, so I wouldn't count it the same way, but I'll note it. The apixaban is the one that matters for bleeding.
FAMILY: Okay. And she's lost weight — about ten pounds over three months, even on that mirtazapine that's supposed to help her eat. She just picks at her food.
DR: That weight loss matters, and I've noted it. So here's where we are. A colonoscopy could find the source of the bleeding — but in someone who is 88, frail, with dementia and on a full-dose blood thinner, the sedation, the prep, and holding her anticoagulation all carry real risk. The referral asked us not to just schedule it, and I agree.
FAMILY: The family wants everything checked, though.
DR: I hear that, and it's a loving instinct. What I'd like to do is not book the scope today, but sit down with you, her cardiologist, and her primary doctor about what she'd want and what the results would change. I also want to be sure she can take part in that decision. Can we do that before committing to anything?
FAMILY: Yes. That's fair. I just want to do right by her.
DR: We will. I'll set up that conversation and we'll decide together.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Beaumont is an 88-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation (on apixaban), moderate dementia, and frailty, referred from primary care for evaluation of new microcytic anemia (Hgb 9.1, down from 11.8) with a positive fecal occult blood test. She resides in assisted living and ambulates with a walker. History is obtained primarily from her daughter, who holds healthcare POA, as the patient has moderate dementia and provides a hedged, unreliable history (she does not recall any bleeding). Daughter reports approximately 10 lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months despite mirtazapine, with reduced appetite. Family expresses a wish to 'have everything checked.'

Medication reconciliation (from daughter with pill bottles): apixaban is actually 5 mg PO BID per the current pharmacy bottle — the chart lists 2.5 mg BID (discrepancy, see plan). Donepezil 10 mg every evening and mirtazapine 15 mg nightly confirmed. Daughter additionally reports OTC fish oil, which she characterized as a 'blood thinner'; this is an over-the-counter omega-3 supplement, not a clinically significant anticoagulant.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 80, RR 16, BP 118/70, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 157 cm (5'2"), Wt 47.2 kg (104 lb), BMI 19.1 (prior Wt ~51.7 kg / 114 lb three months ago, consistent with ~10 lb loss). Labs (2026-07-08): Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, Hematocrit 28.6%, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 11 ug/L (microcytic, iron-deficient pattern); creatinine 1.2 mg/dL. Fecal occult blood positive (2026-06-28). Appears frail; accompanied by daughter/POA.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- New iron-deficiency anemia with guaiac/FOBT-positive stool and unintentional weight loss in an 88-year-old — differential includes occult GI malignancy or other GI blood loss. Colonoscopy is the diagnostic study that would answer the question.
- HOWEVER, procedure is NOT being scheduled today. Given age 88, frailty, moderate dementia, and full-dose anticoagulation, the risks of bowel prep, sedation/anesthesia, and periprocedural anticoagulation interruption are substantial. Per referral flag, a goals-of-care and risk/benefit discussion must precede any decision.
  - Arrange a shared decision-making conversation with the daughter/POA, cardiology, and referring PCP regarding whether findings would change management and what the patient would want.
  - Assess decision-making capacity for informed consent to sedation/procedure; involve POA as surrogate as appropriate.
- Anticoagulation discrepancy: chart lists apixaban 2.5 mg BID but patient is taking 5 mg BID per pill bottle. Reconcile with cardiology — clarify intended dose (note low body weight ~47 kg and age >=80 would ordinarily favor the 2.5 mg reduced dose) and define any periprocedural hold plan only if procedure is pursued.
- Update medication list: correct apixaban dose; add OTC fish oil/omega-3 (clarified as supplement, not therapeutic anticoagulant).
- Iron-deficiency anemia — check iron studies/reticulocytes as needed; consider iron repletion (oral vs IV) independent of procedure decision; monitor Hgb; transfusion threshold per symptoms.
- Weight loss — flag to PCP; nutritional support in assisted living.
- Continue donepezil and mirtazapine.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• A low blood count (anemia) and blood found in a stool test
• Recent weight loss
• Whether a colonoscopy is the right choice for your mother right now
• Her medicines, including her blood thinner

What we decided today
• We are NOT scheduling the colonoscopy yet. Because of your mother's age, frailty, memory condition, and her blood thinner, the prep, sedation, and stopping the blood thinner all carry real risks.
• We will set up a conversation with you (as her healthcare decision-maker), her heart doctor, and her regular doctor to decide together what is best and what she would want.

Medicine notes
• Your mother's records showed apixaban 2.5 mg twice a day, but her bottle shows 5 mg twice a day. We will correct this and check with her heart doctor.
• The fish oil is an over-the-counter supplement, not a true blood thinner like the apixaban. We noted it.
• Keep giving donepezil in the evening and mirtazapine at night.

Next steps
• We will arrange the family/care-team discussion before any procedure.
• We may check iron and treat the low blood count regardless of the decision.
• Please call for any heavy bleeding, black stools, dizziness, fainting, or new weakness.
